title: Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act
short_title: Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (text: CR S231-232)
plain_summary: This bill creates a special Congressional Gold Medal to honor wildland firefighters for their brave work. These firefighters risk their lives to protect America's forests, grasslands, and communities from dangerous fires. The medal will recognize their strength, hard work, and sacrifices. It will be displayed at the National Interagency Fire Center for everyone to see. Bronze copies of the medal can be sold to help pay for making it.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs. (text: CR S231-232)

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Wildland Firefighters Congressional Gold Medal Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned these important facts. Over the past thirty years, wildland fires have become stronger and more destructive. These fires have destroyed homes, roads, bridges, and valuable government and private lands. More than one billion acres of land across the United States could catch fire. About one hundred seventeen million acres of government land have been marked as high risk or very high risk for wildfires. The Forest Service, Bureau of Land Management, National Park Service, United States Fish and Wildlife Service, and Bureau of Indian Affairs together employ more than eighteen thousand seven hundred wildland firefighters. These firefighters battle wildfires across millions of acres of public and private lands each year. Thousands more workers and volunteers serve as state, local, and contract wildland firefighters. As cities and towns expand into wilderness areas, wildfires increasingly move from remote areas into neighborhoods across the United States. Wildland firefighters are learning new skills and methods to fight the growing wildfire threat in the twenty first century. While protecting lives and property remains most important, wildland firefighters also help manage forests responsibly. Wildland firefighters perform controlled burns and other forest care activities including cutting down trees. This work helps keep forests healthy and reduces the risk of catastrophic wildfires. Each wildland firefighter receives special training to work in changing and extremely dangerous conditions. Wildland firefighters regularly work long days during two week work periods, often sleeping in harsh conditions. According to the Forest Service, firefighters generally work sixteen hour days while fighting a fire. They typically work more than two thousand five hundred hours in a six month period. Wildland firefighter crews respond to all kinds of emergencies on the front lines. They use whatever methods necessary to protect lives and property while responding to floods, hurricanes, disease outbreaks, and terrorist attacks. Engine crews and hand crews are the main firefighting workforce. They come in different sizes and can be organized to fit the specific needs and land challenges that each fire presents. Special hotshot crews are highly skilled mobile hand crews with expert knowledge about fire suppression methods. Pilots and aerial fire suppression crews fly in air tankers and helicopters to drop water and fire stopping chemicals to support decision makers on the ground. Firefighters delivered by air include helicopter crews and smokejumpers who exit helicopters and jump from planes into remote and hard to reach areas. They provide quick and targeted fire suppression and emergency medical evacuation by helicopter. These fire personnel provide supervision and direct action on new and ongoing fire incidents. Wildland firefighters in the United States also answer the call to fight wildfires in other countries. During the record setting fires in Australia in two thousand twenty, the United States sent three hundred sixty two firefighters to help. During Canada's historic two thousand twenty three fire season, more than two thousand government wildland firefighters answered the call. As of when this law was passed, the United States maintains mutual help and cooperation agreements for wildland firefighting efforts with Canada, Mexico, Australia, New Zealand, and Portugal. The increases in wildfire severity and in the number of active fire season months each year have also increased the demand for wildland firefighters and related employees. Finding and keeping wildland firefighters has been a national problem for many years. Wildland firefighters put their lives at risk to keep the people of the United States safe, and some pay the ultimate price to do so. Between January first two thousand nineteen and January first two thousand twenty five, ninety eight wildland firefighters have lost their lives fighting fires. Immediate and secondary effects from wildfire such as wildfire smoke exposure are directly linked to tens of thousands of firefighter and civilian deaths each year. June thirtieth to July sixth of each year is recognized as a Week of Remembrance to honor the fallen wildland firefighters who sacrificed their lives to protect the wildlands of the United States. National Wildland Firefighter Day is held every year on July second to recognize all who are devoted to wildland firefighting. The excellent efforts of wildland firefighters deserve recognition, and it is appropriate and proper to honor those who have served before as well as current and future firefighters. Wildland firefighters show principles of duty, respect, and integrity in every aspect of service. Each firefighter shows strength, toughness, and determination to protect the forests, grasslands, and communities of the United States. Wildland firefighters do not run away from dangerous situations but instead risk life and limb to help others. The outstanding accomplishments of these brave individuals continue an unmatched legacy of public service.

sections.title: Section 3 Congressional Gold Medal
sections.text: The Speaker of the House of Representatives and the President pro tempore of the Senate (the person who leads Senate meetings when the Vice President is not there) shall make proper arrangements for giving one gold medal with an appropriate design. This medal will honor wildland firefighters as a group in recognition of their strength, toughness, sacrifice, and service to protect the forests, grasslands, and communities of the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury will make a gold medal with suitable symbols, designs, and words to be decided by the Secretary working with the National Interagency Fire Center (the main coordination center for wildland firefighting). After the gold medal is presented, it will be given to the National Interagency Fire Center where it will be displayed properly and made available for research. It is the opinion of Congress that the National Interagency Fire Center should make sure that the display and availability of the medal happens at appropriate locations, especially locations connected with wildland firefighters.

sections.title: Section 4 Duplicate Medals
sections.text: The Secretary may make and sell bronze copies of the gold medal made under section three at a price high enough to cover all costs including labor, materials, metal stamps, use of machines, and overhead expenses.

sections.title: Section 5 Status of Medals
sections.text: The medals made under this law are national medals for purposes of chapter fifty one of title thirty one United States Code (the part of federal law that covers national medals). For purposes of sections five thousand one hundred thirty four and five thousand one hundred thirty six of title thirty one United States Code, all medals made under this law shall be considered to be collectible coin items.

sections.title: Section 6 Authority to Use Fund Amounts Proceeds of Sale
sections.text: The government is authorized to charge against the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund (the government account that pays for making coins and medals) whatever amounts are necessary to pay for the costs of the medals made under this law. Money received from the sale of duplicate bronze medals authorized under section four shall be put into the United States Mint Public Enterprise Fund.
